figures.caption: Figure 1: Motivation. Under modality-specific shift, biased fusion can produce a sharper but less reliable posterior. Entropy-only adaptation may further amplify an incorrect fused decision, whereas MG-MTTA uses reliability and conflict cues to guide the fusion gate toward a more reliable adaptation direction.

Table 11: Failure-oriented objective ablation under the textual and joint L5 strongest probes. Accuracy is reported as global top-1 accuracy (%). Majorization reports raw averaged majorization ratios; for Source-only, the value equals 1 because no adaptation update is applied and the pre/post posterior is unchanged.

Figure 5: Case-wise analysis under the L5 strongest probe. The examples show recovery under severe textual stress, a lowerentropy but still incorrect prediction, and recovery under high cross-modality conflict. They distinguish genuine recovery from confidence amplification in the modality-dominance regime described in Sec. 3.5.

Table 7: Level-wise results under textual and joint stress. Textual and Joint report level-wise top-1 accuracy (%) for L1–L4. Textual Avg. and Joint Avg. are arithmetic means over L1–L4, and Avg. is the mean of these two averages. The majorization columns report raw L1–L4 averages of the majorization ratio, rather than values normalized to Source-only. We do not report a unified L1–L4 conflict-error aggregate here, because conflict error is used only in the strongest-probe analysis.
Table 8: Hyperparameter ranges screened in pilot runs and final values used in the main experiments.

Figure 2: Overview of MG-MTTA. A frozen vision-language backbone produces modality-level predictions from shifted visual and textual inputs. Running anchors and cross-modality conflict define a reliability-aware prior for a lightweight gate or adapter. The fused prediction is obtained by logit-level convex fusion followed by softmax, and only the lightweight module is updated with entropy, gate regularization, and diversity objectives.
